---
title: "Predicting Outpatient Consultation Duration"
subtitle: "A Leakage-Safe ML Pipeline for TDABC-Informed Scheduling"
author: "Ariana Butina"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    incremental: false
    fig-width: 6.5
    fig-height: 4
    embed-resources: true
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
import json
from pathlib import Path

from IPython.display import Image

ARTIFACTS = Path("../ml/artifacts")


def load_json(name: str) -> dict:
    with open(ARTIFACTS / name) as f:
        return json.load(f)


clf_metrics = load_json("test_metrics_classification.json")
reg_metrics = load_json("test_metrics_regression.json")
clf_tuning = load_json("tuning_results_classification.json")
metadata = load_json("metadata.json")
```

## Motivation {.smaller}

Time-Driven Activity-Based Costing needs one core input per activity: **how long it
takes.**

- 6,637 historical outpatient oncology consultations
- Two models: **will this visit be long?** and **how many minutes will it take?**
- Built to support a master's thesis applying TDABC to outpatient scheduling
- Design priority throughout: **no leakage, no dishonest evaluation** — even where the
  less impressive choice was the correct one

## System Architecture

```
GitHub  ──▶  Render (FastAPI + scikit-learn)  ◀── fetch() ──  Vercel (React + Recharts)
  │
  └──▶  ml/ pipeline: load → clean → engineer → select → split → transform
                        → tune → evaluate → explain → persist
```

Each arrow below is one pipeline stage — and one module in `ml/`.

## `ml/data/load.py` — Load

- **`load_raw_data(path, validate=True)`** — reads `Data.csv`, returns a `DataFrame`
- **`_validate_schema(df)`** — asserts every expected column is present with the
  expected dtype *before* anything downstream touches the data

::: {.notes}
Fail fast: a silently-missing or renamed column should break loading, not corrupt a
model three steps later.
:::

## `ml/data/clean.py` — Clean {.smaller}

- **`fill_missing_address(df)`** — fills missing `Address` with an explicit
  `"Unknown"` category (missingness is plausibly meaningful, not random)
- **`validate_categorical_domains(df)`** — asserts categorical columns only contain
  expected values
- **`check_no_duplicate_rows(df)`** — guards against accidental double-counting
- **`report_patient_label_inconsistency(df)`** — *reports*, but does not silently
  "fix," the 33 patients whose `M.Cancer` flag changes across visits
- **`clean_data(df)`** — orchestrates the above into one call

## `ml/data/features.py` — Engineer {.smaller}

- **`add_service_time_minutes(df)`** — seconds → minutes (both targets derive from this)
- **`compute_long_consultation_threshold(df)`** — the dataset-wide median, computed
  **once** and frozen as a business-rule constant
- **`add_long_consultation_label(df, threshold)`** — applies that frozen threshold
- **`add_repeat_visit_flag`**, **`add_public_holiday_makeup_flag`**,
  **`add_combined_cancer_flag`** — the three engineered features, each mapping
  directly to an EDA finding
- **`engineer_features(df, threshold=None)`** — orchestrates all of the above

## `ml/data/feature_selection.py` — Select {.smaller}

- **`get_classification_features()`** / **`get_regression_features()`** — the frozen,
  shared 12-column predictor list
- **`_validate_no_leakage(feature_list, target_column)`** — asserts neither target,
  nor any column that perfectly determines it, appears in the predictor list
- **`select_classification_data(df)`** / **`select_regression_data(df)`** — return
  `(X, y)`, leakage-checked

## `ml/data/split.py` — Split

- **`grouped_stratified_split(df, ...)`** — `StratifiedGroupKFold`, grouped by
  patient `ID`, stratified by `IsLongConsultation`
- **`assert_no_group_leakage(train, test, group_col)`** — asserts zero patient overlap
  between train and test — not a comment, an executable check
- **`create_train_test_split()`** / **`save_split()`** / **`get_train_test_split()`** —
  build once, persist to `data/processed/`, reload without re-splitting

## `ml/preprocessing/column_transformer.py` — Transform

- **`build_classification_transformer()`** / **`build_regression_transformer()`** —
  one shared `ColumnTransformer`: median-impute + scale the numeric feature,
  most-frequent-impute the booleans, most-frequent-impute + one-hot
  (`handle_unknown="ignore"`) the categoricals
- Always **fit on the training fold only** — never on the full dataset

## `ml/training/cv_utils.py` + `search.py` — Tune {.smaller}

- **`get_classification_cv()`** / **`get_regression_cv()`** — group-aware CV splitters
- **`assert_cv_has_no_group_leakage(...)`** — the same leakage check, enforced again
  at the CV-fold level
- **`run_randomized_search(pipeline, param_grid, cv, ...)`** — `RandomizedSearchCV`
  wrapper, seeded
- **`save_tuning_results()`** / **`load_tuning_results()`** /
  **`build_best_pipeline_from_results()`** — persist and rehydrate tuning output

## `ml/training/train_classifier.py` / `train_regressor.py` {.smaller}

- **`tune_classifier(model_name, ...)`** / **`tune_regressor(model_name, ...)`** —
  one call per candidate model family
- **`tune_all_classifiers()`** / **`tune_all_regressors()`** — runs Logistic/Ridge,
  Random Forest, and XGBoost through the same search
- **`select_best_classifier()`** / **`select_best_regressor()`** — picks the winner by
  CV score — **`random_forest` won both tasks**

## Tuning Results

```{python}
#| label: tbl-tuning
import pandas as pd

rows = [{"Model": k.replace("_", " ").title(), "CV ROC-AUC": round(v["best_score"], 4)}
        for k, v in clf_tuning.items() if k != "best_model"]
pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("CV ROC-AUC", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)
```

All three model families land within ~0.003 ROC-AUC of each other — the feature set's
signal ceiling, not the model choice, is the binding constraint.

## `ml/evaluation/metrics.py` + `evaluate_*.py` — Evaluate, Once, Honestly {.smaller}

- **`classification_metrics()`** / **`regression_metrics()`** — accuracy, precision,
  recall, F1, ROC-AUC / MAE, RMSE, R²
- **`plot_confusion_matrix()`**, **`plot_roc_curve()`**, **`plot_residuals()`** —
  the exact figures on the next slides
- **`evaluate_classifier_on_test_set()`** / **`evaluate_regressor_on_test_set()`** —
  refit on full train, score on held-out test — **exactly once**
- **`run_classification_evaluation()`** / **`run_regression_evaluation()`** —
  orchestrate + save metrics as JSON artifacts (the numbers on this deck come
  straight from those files, not from re-running anything)

## `ml/evaluation/explainability.py` — Explain

- **`get_feature_names(pipeline)`** — recovers human-readable names after one-hot
  encoding
- **`get_feature_importance(pipeline)`** — Random Forest's built-in importances
- **`compute_shap_values(pipeline, X)`** — SHAP values for a deeper, per-feature view
- **`plot_feature_importance()`** / **`plot_shap_summary()`** — the figures used in
  the report and dashboard

## `ml/persistence.py` — Persist, Reproducibly {.smaller}

- **`save_pipeline()`** / **`load_pipeline()`** — joblib serialization of the fitted
  `Pipeline` (preprocessing + model together, one artifact)
- **`get_git_commit_hash()`** — stamps the exact commit that produced an artifact
- **`get_library_versions()`** — records pandas/scikit-learn/numpy versions
- **`build_metadata()`** — writes `metadata.json`: seed, features, best params, test
  metrics, commit hash, library versions — everything needed to explain *why* a number
  is what it is, months later

## Results — Classification

```{python}
#| label: fig-confusion-matrix
#| fig-cap: "Confusion matrix (held-out test set)"
Image("figures/confusion_matrix.png")
```

ROC-AUC = `{python} f"{clf_metrics['roc_auc']:.3f}"` — better than chance, far from decisive.

## Results — Regression

```{python}
#| label: fig-residuals
#| fig-cap: "Residuals vs. predicted (held-out test set)"
Image("figures/residuals.png")
```

R² = `{python} f"{reg_metrics['r2']:.3f}"` — long visits are systematically underestimated.

## Feature Importance

```{python}
#| label: fig-feature-importance
#| fig-cap: "Random Forest feature importances (classification)"
Image("figures/feature_importance_classification.png")
```

`Visit.No` and `IsRepeatVisit` dominate — far ahead of diagnosis or calendar features.

## What This Means for TDABC

- **Visit history — not diagnosis, not day-of-week — is the dominant driver of
  consultation length.**
- Segment cost-driver rates by **new vs. returning patient**, not by calendar or
  diagnosis category.
- Treat predictions as a **capacity-planning aid**, not a precise per-slot scheduler or
  clinical signal.

## Honest Limitations

- Regression explains only a small share of duration variance — the available fields
  are scheduling metadata, not clinical complexity
- Single clinic, 2018–2019 — generalisation to other clinics/specialties is untested
- 33 patients have an inconsistent `M.Cancer` flag across visits, left uncorrected by
  design
- `S.Cancer` is rare (~1%) — its individual contribution is hard to estimate reliably

## Live Demo

- **App:** [frontend-psi-seven-76.vercel.app](https://frontend-psi-seven-76.vercel.app)
- **API docs:** [hangu-consult-backend.onrender.com/docs](https://hangu-consult-backend.onrender.com/docs)
- **Source:** [github.com/arianabutina-source/hangu-consult-time-ml](https://github.com/arianabutina-source/hangu-consult-time-ml)

## Conclusion

A fully reproducible, leakage-audited pipeline — every stage tested, every evaluation
run exactly once — turns ordinary scheduling data into a genuinely useful, honestly
limited signal for outpatient capacity planning and TDABC cost-driver estimation.

**Thank you.**
